items.title: Welcome!
items.text: This is my personal website, with all the cool and weird things I bring with me.

Needs configuration

Note that MathJax is included via a script tag on this page and is not configured in the generated default configuration to avoid including it in a pages that do not need it. See the documentation for details on how to configure it on all your pages if they are more Maths-heavy than these simple starter pages.

items.title: Projects
items.text: Welcome to this section, where all my personal projects are presented.

items.title: VintageCam
items.text:
This project is about making a camcorder-style camera with real analog signal noise coming from the Composite Video Baseband Signal (CVBS) connection.

Learned

  • How does CVBS (NTSC/PAL) work
  • Differences between NTSC and PAL
  • How to capture video using ffmpeg
  • Introduction to Buildroot

Other tools used:

  • make
  • dd
  • ssh
  • rsync

Hanover Bars with PAL delay

items.title: Requirements
items.text: The goal is a device, which boots quickly and uses buttons for operating. The camera captures NTSC or PAL video to the internal storage and has a way to quickly transfer the footage to a computer for video editing.

items.title: Ingredients
items.text:
• FPV camera with CVBS analog output (ideally with CCD sensor) (EACHINE 1000TVL CCD)
  • Raspberry Pi 5
  • LogiLink (VG0001) USB Audio & Video Grabber
  • Powerbank (hama Supreme10HD)

items.title: The Plan
items.text:
The plan is to set up the following pipeline: 

flowchart LR
  A(CCD camera) e1@===>|CVBS| B(LogiLink);
  B e2@==> C(Raspberry Pi 5);
  e1@{ animation: fast }
  e2@{ animation: fast }

The Pi 5 shall run on a customized linux image, such as to reduce boot time and improve efficiency.

items.title: Step 1: Testing the camera and dongle
items.text: I connected the camera with the video grabber and connected it to my computer. The device was immediately recognizable in OBS: Success! There is video!

items.title: Step 2: Finding the right ffmpeg command
items.text:
After testing a bunch of different ffmpeg commands, I finally found the right flags to get my desired result. I looked at deinterlacing, digital compression and other hardware-related configurations.

Before I went on, I tested the command on the target device, which was a Raspberry Pi 2B at the time. I quickly realized its computational power was not enough for this amount of data from the LogiLink video grabber.

items.title: Step 3: Writing a Python script
items.text: Now that I had a ffmpeg capture command, I needed to setup a Python script to handle starting/stopping via GPIO input and showing the recording status with a small blinking light.

items.title: Step 4: Buildroot
items.text: After establishing that the python script works, I set off to learn about Buildroot after deciding between it and Yocto due to the simplicity for nice and quick configurations. Buildroot is a really useful tool for making custom single-purpose linux images. You can make a relatively small (~100MB) OS-image, and it has a much faster booting time compared to using a full linux distribution since we can preinstall only the required packages. On top of that we can easily configure scripts for automatically starting a process after booting finished.

items.title: Installation
items.text: To install Buildroot, I simply downloaded the latest version from the official page and installed all required prerequisites.

items.title: Buildroot Basics
items.text:
A simple command as

make menuconfig

is used to enter the configuration editor.

To make life easier, there are predefined "defconfig" files for popular boards such as the Raspberry Pi 5. To display all available configurations, run:

make list-defconfigs

When we know the name for our board, we can load it in with

make raspberrypi5-defconfig

To save our own changes to the configuration, we have to save the current configuration to a new defconfig-file and then load our custom defconfig so we don't accidentally overwrite the official Pi 5 defconfig. I ran these commands:

make savedefconfig BR2_DEFCONFIG=configs/my_pi5_defconfig
make my_pi5_defconfig
# from this point saving with just
make savedefconfig

Now if we enter menuconfig we have prepared default configurations for the Raspberry Pi 5. However, as of writing this, there is a missing configuration to get a successful boot:

Navigate to 

Target packages -> Hardware handling -> Firmware
 -> rpi-firmware -> [*] Install DTB overlays (enable it as seen here)

After exiting the menuconfig and applying changes, we can start compiling our basic image.

make
# or
make all

People say it may take 2-3 hours, but my Tuxedo laptop took just about 25 minutes.

items.title: Flashing the SD-Card
items.text:
To run our compiled system on the Pi 5, we have to flash the SD-card. I plugged it in an used dd to copy the image file:

Warning

Be careful not to overwrite the wrong storage device!

lsblk # lists all storage devices, in my case mmcblk0 is the name of my SD-card
sudo dd if=output/images/sdcard.img of=/dev/mmcblk0

By default there is no progress information on the copy process, but the it takes me just about 13 seconds on average. Patience. Hopefully after inserting the SD-card into the Pi 5, and connecting the device to power (and a monitor), you should successfully boot.

items.title: Additional Packages
items.text:
Great! We have a minimal OS as a basis. Now we can start customizing the configurations to our needs in menuconfig. Most importantly, we have to install some packages. In Target Packages you should find all the available packages. My recommendation for testing are ssh functionality and an automatic network connection via WIFI:

  • dropbear # an ssh server
  • avahi # so we can connect with name.local instead of the IPv4 address
  • wps-component # for WIFI //TODO

items.title: Mini-keyboard Holder
items.text: As a Yamaha Reface owner, although I love the small keys, sometimes they are not enough for me. That's when I have the option to connect it to a larger keyboard via MIDI. However, I am not quite satisfied with my setup, requiring a table or chair nearby to place the Reface, since placing it on the keyboard itself is pretty inconvenient and quite frankly dangerous. Since mini-keyboard stands are quite expensive and require a fitting larger stand, I decided to build my own portable extention. That way I should be able to mount my bigger keyboard on any keyboard I want, no matter the keyboardstand.

items.title: Requirements
items.text: The holder should be portable with the Reface, be independent of the base keyboard stand architecture, stable and simple to install.

items.title: Ingredients
items.text:
• 4x 90° angle metal plate corners
  • 2x screws with flat, plastic head
  • 2x nuts for the screws
  • 3D-printed parts

items.title: The Plan
items.text:
The idea is to have a clamp on either side of the Reface, attached with a 3D-printed mounting for the Reface. The clamps should attach to the bigger keyboard from top and bottom. A screw on the bottom tightens the grip and also allows for easy mounting.

Two angles connected in a U-shape provide the base for the clamp. The inside of one clamp arm has an attached 3D-printed profile adjusted to the bigger keyboards inclination against the bottom. The opposite arm has a winding for the screw.
